This is for heature gtest

Now let’s move on to the next feature: organization feature gates.

We already have a placeholder system, but here’s how it actually needs to work.
Every time a user performs an action that consumes something from the organization, we first run the normal permission checks, then immediately check the feature gates based on the organization’s tier. If the gate passes, the action continues. If it fails, the action is blocked before it reaches any deeper logic.

The part I’m unsure about is the best way to store and track consumption with precision in the database while also caching it. We don’t want to query the database every time just to see how much the org has used. Instead, after each consumption event, we should invalidate both the server and client caches and replace them with the new values. That keeps both sides consistently up to date.

One important note: when retrieving limits, we should always rely on the single source of truth in the app. Everything must be fully typed to match the features defined in that source of truth so the server and client stay in sync with zero ambiguity.

Also remember: we need to save and cache the organization/studio tier details too. That way we can inject that into the context and speed up the procedures that rely on it.

- A desktop icon show if the element is only visible on desktop devices

The second line shows through because the overflow property is set to visible. As a result, all the other elements on the page that are right below this text don't follow the natural flow of the page. This is affecting desktop, mobile, and the preview. The fix is to remove minHeight from the content div and let it flow naturally so the parent wrapper grows to accommodate the content.

There is a bug in the padding control in the properties panel. The user cannot toggle the option to add individual values for different sides. It only shows one value at all times. This should look and feel like Figma. The same issue exists for the margin control.

---

CSS Formatting Bug - Missing px Units in Multi-Value Shorthand Properties

The spacing and border-radius controls were not updating the canvas element state. The root cause was that the format functions (formatSpacingValue, formatBorderRadiusValue) returned values like "10 20 30 40" without px units. CSS requires units for shorthand values - they must be "10px 20px 30px 40px". Single numeric values work fine in React's style object, but multi-value strings need explicit units. Fix the format functions to include px units when returning multiple values.

Please follow the official security advisory guide at https://nextjs.org/blog/CVE-2025-66478 to fix this vulnerability. This is a CVSS 10.0 remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in React Server Components that affects the App Router. The patched version for our version line is 16.0.7.

AI Agent Orchestration Analysis and Refactoring

We have built an AI orchestrating agent inside our app that helps us create stuff in the database. However, this is super complicated and may not even work for production. Some of the things are not straightforward, and everything is all over the place. The streaming setup also does not work as we need it to, and I think we might have manually created some stuff instead of using Trigger.dev natively.

The way it's supposed to work is the AI agent is supposed to decide what tools to call and it's also supposed to get context of what it's done accordingly. But with our setup, because we're manually doing all of this through a for loop through a wave pattern, we are faking AI orchestration to create parallelism.

I want you to analyze and look at Trigger.dev and see exactly what they're doing. I want you to read their documentation and deeply analyze and scrutinize our code. You have to give a different perspective on what we have written. If you think that the things we've actually done are horrible and incorrect, please give us a new solution. The end goal here is I want source of truth and simpler AI agent stuff so I can add to it because right now there's way too much everywhere.

---

Refactor Mochi AI Agent to Use Trigger.dev ai.tool() Pattern

The current implementation has a large tools.ts file (1500+ lines) with BOTH planning tools (Vercel AI SDK) AND execution tasks (schemaTask) defined separately. There's a manual TOOL_REGISTRY that maps tool names to tasks, and the agent manually extracts tool calls and batch executes them. This creates duplication and complexity.

Refactor to use Trigger.dev's native ai.tool() pattern to eliminate this duplication. Create a new file structure under src/lib/trigger/mochi/tools/ with domain-specific tool files:

1. shared.ts - tRPC client factory, context extraction, and error handling utilities
2. leads.ts - Lead management tools (createLead, listLeads, getLead, updateLead, deleteLead, countLeads)
3. tags.ts - Tag management tools (createTag, listTags, addTagToLead, removeTagFromLead)
4. datasets.ts - Dataset tools (createDataset, listDatasets, getDataset, updateDataset, deleteDataset, createDatasetField, createDatasetWithFields, addLeadsToDataset)
5. products.ts - Product management tools (createProduct, listProducts, getProduct, updateProduct, deleteProduct)
6. prices.ts - Price and feature tools (createPrice, updatePrice, deletePrice, setFeatures)
7. payment-links.ts - Payment link tools (createPaymentLink, getPaymentLinks, deactivatePaymentLink)
8. index.ts - Export all AI tools as MOCHI_TOOLS object and all tasks for direct triggering

Each tool should be defined as a schemaTask with proper description and schema fields using .describe() for AI context. Then wrap with ai.tool() for direct use in generateText(). This eliminates the need for separate planning tools and the manual TOOL_REGISTRY - the schema and description from the schemaTask are automatically used for the AI tool definition.

---

Add Real-Time Streaming Support to Mochi AI Agent

The current frontend uses useRealtimeRun() which only provides metadata updates, not text streaming. AI responses are only shown after completion. I want to add real-time streaming support using Trigger.dev's native streams feature.

Create a new file src/lib/trigger/mochi/streams.ts that defines streams for the Mochi AI agent. This should include:

1. A stream for the AI's text response - used to stream the final response text to the frontend in real-time
2. A stream for tool execution progress - used to stream updates about which tools are being executed

The streams should be defined using Trigger.dev's streams.define() API (requires SDK v4.1.0+). Include proper TypeScript types so the frontend can use useRealtimeRunWithStreams() hook with full type safety.

In the agent task, you pipe a stream to the defined stream using stream.pipe(). On the frontend, you subscribe using useRealtimeRunWithStreams() and access the streamed parts array.

Also update the types.ts file if needed to add streaming-related metadata fields.

---

Simplify Mochi AI Agent Using Native Vercel AI SDK Patterns

The current agent.ts file (666 lines) has become overly complex with manual agentic loop handling. It uses a manual while loop with MAX_ITERATIONS = 10, manually extracts tool calls from planResult.steps, manually builds batch execution payloads via batch.triggerByTaskAndWait(), and manually constructs conversation context.

The tools have already been refactored to use the ai.tool() pattern and are exported from src/lib/trigger/mochi/tools/index.ts as MOCHI_TOOLS.

Create a new simplified agent.ts file that:

1. Uses MOCHI_TOOLS or creates planning tools directly with Vercel AI SDK's tool() function - no manual tool call extraction needed
2. Uses maxSteps: 10 for native loop control instead of a manual while loop
3. Keeps the human-in-the-loop (askUser) functionality with wait.forToken()
4. Keeps metadata updates for frontend progress tracking via metadata.set()
5. Keeps fatal error detection for FORBIDDEN/UNAUTHORIZED errors
6. Uses batch.triggerByTaskAndWait() for parallel tool execution with TOOL_TASKS mapping

The key architectural insight is that planning tools (what the AI sees) are separate from execution tasks (what Trigger.dev runs). Planning tools are lightweight tool() definitions without \_ctx. Execution tasks are schemaTask definitions with \_ctx for authenticated API calls. The agent maps from planning tool calls to execution task payloads by injecting \_ctx.

This separation keeps the AI interface clean (it does not need to know about authentication tokens) while maintaining security (all actual API calls go through authenticated tasks).

AI Agent Website Builder Canvas Integration

I want to add a feature where the AI agent can build websites on the canvas. This is complex because our canvas has very specific properties and types for each style and element type. Build a simple system that we can expand later, but it should be capable of creating frames and following our architecture.

The AI agent needs to understand how to build elements on the canvas. Look at the existing Neon MCP mock data in the database for inspiration on structure. Don't copy this directly and tell the AI to always build like this - use it as an example to help guide the AI through prompts so it knows how to build elements.

The most important part is providing context to the user. If the user is on a specific website page (we call those pages or scenes), you need to provide that data to the AI agent. Build this out start to finish, but be very minimal and keep the architecture small. Follow the current architecture in place.

We have a save canvas server action, but this saves the entire page itself. As long as agents have correct schemas to protect this, they can only create data in a very specific format. Unless that's the case, the agent should not be able to create data. This is the most critical part - no matter what the agent creates, the schema should be a one-to-one representation of only the properties we have and only the types of elements we have. Nothing outside of this.

Implementation approach:

1. Created strict Zod schemas that match our exact canvas element types (frame, text, image, button)
2. Only allowed properties that exist in our BaseElement and element-specific interfaces
3. Used discriminated unions for type safety
4. Canvas context is passed from frontend to agent when user is on a canvas page
5. Context includes pageId, domainId, websiteId, pageName, pageSlug, and current canvasData
6. Agent prompt includes guidance on common patterns (hero, navbar, card) and style best practices
7. Tools save via the same tRPC endpoint as auto-save (upsert pattern)

---

AI Agent Canvas Context Optimization - Selection-Based Context

Passing the entire canvas data with all elements, root IDs, and children map is too expensive. Instead, use a selection-based approach:

1. If user has an element selected (frame, page, etc.), pass only that element's data so the AI can modify it or add children to it
2. If nothing is selected, send only page/org context without element data - AI builds at a calculated position
3. Use viewport data (pan/zoom) to calculate where the user is looking - place new elements at center of viewport
4. Show "smart context" in the chatbot UI - display what's selected so user knows they can ask about that element
5. Dynamic placeholder text - change based on selection: "What do you want to add to this frame?" or "What do you want to build?"
6. Don't default to page element - user might want to create a standalone card component on the canvas to experiment with

The key insight is that context should be minimal and targeted. Only send what's needed for the current operation, and let the user's selection guide what the AI can modify.

---

AI Agent Canvas Simplification - Remove Selection Logic

The AI agent for the website builder was getting too complicated with the selection-based context. The AI was trying to find existing elements to place children inside, but this caused bugs where:

1. Selected element IDs from the frontend didn't exist in the database (sync issues)
2. Parent-child relationships broke and created orphaned elements
3. The whole page/scene could be destroyed if the parent ID was invalid

Simplified approach:

1. Remove all selectedElement logic from the canvas context
2. AI always creates a ROOT frame at the viewport position
3. All child elements go inside that root frame using tempId/parentTempId references within the batch
4. No references to existing canvas elements - keeps it simple and safe
5. The tool validates that the first element must be a frame - errors if not
6. All subsequent elements must have a parentTempId referencing something in the same batch

This ensures:

- Predictable behavior - everything goes in one root frame at viewport center
- No orphaned elements - all children are inside the batch's root frame
- Easy to test - just check if root frame appears at viewport position
- No dependency on existing canvas state for parent relationships

FreshHarvest Website Design Recreation in Canvas Builder

1. Do NOT build inside the existing PAGE element - create a separate root frame positioned to the right of the existing page (x: 1600)
2. Build section by section because the design is too large to do all at once
3. Use Unsplash placeholder images for all imagery
4. All text elements must have autoWidth: true property set
5. Match the design exactly with potential improvements
6. Don't reference existing content - start fresh with the FreshHarvest design

The sections to build are:

- Header/Navbar with logo, navigation links (Fresh Fruits, Organic Vegetables, Seasonal, Recipes), cart icon, and Order Now button
- Hero section with headline "Farm Fresh. Naturally Delicious. Delivered Daily.", subtext, two CTA buttons, and a fruit image

- Product grid section with 4 product cards (strawberries, blueberries, oranges, avocados) each with image, name, description, price, and "Customize & Add" button
- Why Choose section with 3 feature cards (Farm Fresh Daily, 100% Organic, Fast Delivery)

Each element needs proper parent-child relationships stored in childrenMap. The design uses a cream/warm color palette (#FDF6F0 background) with orange accent color (#E07B4C) for buttons and highlights.

Note: During implementation, discovered that some pages use an older flat canvas structure (rootIds, elements, childrenMap at top level) vs newer scenes-based structure (scenes array with sceneOrder and activeSceneId). This may cause rendering issues if the builder expects one format but receives the other.

Website Builder Styles vs Settings Refactoring

The website builder has two different things called properties and styles, which creates confusion. Styles are technically a subset of properties anyway. Everything that shows in the Design tab should be a style. Some elements have custom styles (like frame has layout stuff, image doesn't have justify center), and that's fine.

Properties should be renamed to settings and represent element-specific configurations. For example, a future navigation bar component would have settings like nav links, page locations, etc. Settings are unique to each element type and control behavior, not appearance.

The refactoring approach:

1. Styles = ALL visual/CSS properties (including typography: fontSize, fontFamily, fontWeight, lineHeight, letterSpacing, textAlign, color)
2. Settings = Element-specific behavior configurations (autoWidth, sticky, responsive, container, visible, locked, variant, objectFit, content for text, src/alt for image, label for button)

This creates a clear separation:

- Styles affect how things LOOK (can be spread directly to CSS)
- Settings affect how things BEHAVE (require custom logic per element type)

Properties Panel Duplicate Sections Bug Fix

I see two Dimensions sections for text elements and also see an empty Spacing and Border section (doesn't need this category at all). The issue was that:

1. There was a general Dimensions section that showed for ALL elements, plus a separate dedicated Dimensions section specifically for text elements - causing duplicate sections
2. The Spacing & Borders section was always rendered but all the controls inside checked for frame/page type only, so for text and button elements it rendered as an empty section

Fix: Wrap the general Dimensions section to only show for frame, page, and image elements (text elements have their own dedicated section). Also wrap the entire Spacing & Borders section to only render for frame and page elements, since text and button elements don't need gap, padding, margin, corners, or border controls.

Button Spacing Controls and Default Values

Can you add the spacing and border stuff for buttons please. And make sure you also set some default values for the buttons because right now it's always stuck with no padding etc.

Fix: Added a dedicated "Spacing" section for button elements that includes:

1. Padding control - inner spacing using SpacingControl (same Figma-style linked/unlinked UI as frames)
2. Margin control - outer spacing when button is inside a parent frame

The default button styles already had `padding: '12px 24px'` defined in DEFAULT_BUTTON_STYLES in types.ts. The properties panel now reads this value and allows users to modify it. The parseSpacingValue function correctly handles CSS shorthand strings like '12px 24px' (vertical horizontal format).

---

Button Auto-Width Default Sizing

The button is always taking a hardcoded width. By default, set it to take width (inner space) based on content. The user can obviously update the width later.

Fix: Added `autoWidth` and `autoHeight` boolean properties to ButtonElement interface (similar to TextElement). When these are true, the button sizes itself based on content (padding + text) instead of using fixed dimensions.

Implementation:

1. Added `autoWidth?: boolean` and `autoHeight?: boolean` to ButtonElement interface in types.ts
2. Updated DEFAULT_BUTTON_PROPS to set `autoWidth: true` and `autoHeight: true` by default
3. Updated button-element.tsx to check these properties and use `width: 'auto'` / `height: 'auto'` when enabled
4. Updated computeButtonContentStyles in style-utils.ts to use relative positioning when auto-sizing (so content defines size)
5. Updated ButtonElementRenderer in page-renderer.tsx for preview mode
6. Updated canvas.tsx to include autoWidth/autoHeight when creating new buttons

Backwards compatible: Existing buttons without these properties default to false, using their fixed width/height values.

---

Container Mode Not Rendering on Canvas

The container property was showing up in the preview render view but not on the canvas editor. When container mode is enabled, children should be wrapped in a centered max-width container (1280px).

Fix: Added container mode support to frame-element.tsx (canvas view) to match page-renderer.tsx (preview view):

1. Added `hasContainer` check that reads the `container` property:

   - Pages default to true (container !== false)
   - Frames default to false (container === true)

2. Modified the content div to conditionally wrap children in a container wrapper when enabled:
   - Container wrapper has: width: 100%, maxWidth: 1280px, margin: auto, display: flex
   - Inherits flexDirection, justifyContent, alignItems, and gap from parent content styles
   - When container is OFF, children render directly without wrapper

This ensures the canvas editor and preview renderer display container mode identically.

---

PreBuilt Elements System - Navbar Component Architecture

I want to build the PreBuilt elements section in the Insert tab. Each PreBuilt elements section should have subsections like accordions for subcategories (e.g., nav bars, FAQs, hero sections). For now, focus on the navbar elements only, with just one variant - this is for architecture setup that we can expand later.

The key challenge is creating a system that gives the AI the ability to use these different types of elements quickly. The architecture should include descriptions, helpers, keywords, tags, and niche titles attached to each PreBuilt object so AI can understand when and how to use them as references in the future.

The most important part: navbar elements are NON-FREELY EDITABLE. This means:

- Users cannot remove or delete any children elements inside the navbar
- Users cannot drag around child elements or remove them from the flow
- Children are not selectable or hoverable - only the entire navbar can be selected
- Users can drag the parent navbar element, but cannot modify individual children

Configuration happens through the Settings panel in the right sidebar. This Settings panel (separate from the Design panel for visual properties) is where users:

- Add, remove, and reorder navigation links
- Configure the logo (text, image, or both)
- Set up the CTA button
- Configure sticky behavior
- Adjust mobile menu settings

Each PreBuilt element type will have different settings properties. The navbar settings include logo configuration, navigation links array, optional CTA button, sticky behavior, and mobile menu settings.

For mobile responsiveness, use a shadcn Sheet component. When the navbar container shrinks to mobile size, show a hamburger/sidebar trigger inside the navbar. Clicking this opens a Sheet/Drawer showing all the configured navigation links. This creates a responsive version automatically.

Users can update styles for the entire navbar (background, colors, etc.) through the Design panel, but cannot change individual child element properties - the navbar is rock-solid structurally.

Build this minimally but ensure the architecture allows for:

- Easy addition of new PreBuilt element types (hero, footer, FAQ, etc.)
- Easy addition of new variants for each type
- AI-friendly metadata (tags, keywords, use cases, niches)
- Proper type safety throughout

---

## PreBuilt Elements - Design Panel Styles Support

There was no way for users to update styles on the main PreBuilt element itself. The Design panel needs to show the typical style controls (background, padding, borders, etc.) for the entire navbar element. Custom PreBuilt components can be edited at the root level only - the main parent element. This is fine and expected behavior.

The solution is to add PreBuilt elements to all the relevant Design panel sections:

- Dimensions section (size controls)
- Background section (color, image)
- Spacing & Borders section (gap, padding, margin, corners, border)
- Options section (visible, locked)

This gives users full control over the container styling while keeping the internal structure locked.

---

## PreBuilt Elements - Auto Width Behavior

By default, the navbar element needs to have auto-width behavior, otherwise it will never trigger the mobile menu and responsive versions. However, the implementation needs to be smart:

- When on the canvas (root level, parentId is null): Use fixed default width so the element does not collapse in on itself
- When inside a frame or page (parentId is not null): Automatically become 100% width for responsive behavior

This is hardcoded behavior, not a user toggle. The navbar automatically fills its parent container when placed inside one. This way the element works properly both when floating on canvas and when placed inside a page structure.

---

## PreBuilt Elements - Preview and Live Mode Rendering

All custom PreBuilt elements need to render in the preview renderer and live mode (page-renderer.tsx). This component is used for:

- Preview mode overlay in the builder
- Published website rendering
- Thumbnail generation
- Export/print views

The solution is to:

1. Import the PreBuilt types and type guards in page-renderer.tsx
2. Add a case in the ElementRenderer function for `element.type === 'prebuilt'`
3. Create dedicated preview renderer components (e.g., PreBuiltNavbarRenderer) with:
   - No selection/hover states
   - No drag handlers
   - Fully functional interactions (links work, mobile menu opens)
   - Proper responsive behavior using CSS media queries

The preview renderer uses a CSS-only approach for the mobile menu (details/summary pattern) to avoid dependencies on shadcn Sheet component, making it lightweight and self-contained.

---

## Auto-Save Bug Fix - Comparison Key Timing

There was a critical bug in the auto-save hook where saving would never actually happen. The console would show "Scene changed, scheduling save" followed immediately by "Scene unchanged, skipping save".

The bug was caused by updating the comparison key BEFORE the debounce timer fired:

1. Change detected → comparison key updated immediately
2. Debounce timer scheduled
3. When debounce fires, executeSave compares current vs last key
4. But we already updated the last key in step 1, so they match
5. Save is skipped because it thinks nothing changed

The fix: Use TWO separate refs for comparison keys:

1. `lastSavedSceneStatesRef` - Updated ONLY after a successful save. Used by `executeSave` to determine if there's actually something to save.

2. `pendingComparisonKeyRef` - Updated immediately when a change is detected. Used by `handleChange` to prevent duplicate "scene changed" logs during the debounce period.

The `handleChange` callback now checks both:

- If `currentKey === lastSavedKey` → no change since last save, skip entirely
- If `currentKey === pendingKey` → same change already scheduled, skip (prevents 700 logs during viewport pans)

This prevents duplicate logs while still allowing the save to proceed correctly.

---

## PreBuilt Navbar - Container Query Responsive Fix

There were several issues with the navbar's responsive behavior:

1. **Hamburger menu not showing mobile menu when clicked**: The mobile drawer in preview/live mode wasn't opening. The menu button needed proper container query CSS.

2. **Navbar styles not persisting to preview canvas state**: The preview renderer (page-renderer.tsx) wasn't properly passing the `isMobileView` prop to sub-components.

3. **Responsive view using viewport instead of container**: The navbar was using Tailwind's `md:hidden` and `hidden md:block` classes which respond to VIEWPORT width. In our canvas builder, elements need to respond to their CONTAINER width because:
   - The canvas page might be 1440px wide but placed in a mobile breakpoint preview frame (375px)
   - Viewport-based classes would still show desktop layout because the browser window is large
   - Container queries respond to the parent container width, which is what we want

**Solution**: Replace all viewport-based media queries with container queries:

1. Added `containerType: 'inline-size'` to the navbar wrapper in both:

   - `prebuilt-navbar.tsx` (canvas editor)
   - `page-renderer.tsx` (preview/live mode)

2. Replaced viewport-based classes and CSS:

   - `md:hidden` → `@container (max-width: ${breakpoint}px)` CSS
   - `hidden md:block` → `@container (min-width: ${breakpoint}px)` CSS
   - `@media (max-width: ...)` → `@container (max-width: ...)` in preview components

3. Created unique CSS class names to avoid conflicts:

   - `navbar-desktop-links-editor` for canvas
   - `navbar-cta-editor` for canvas
   - `navbar-mobile-menu-editor` for canvas

4. Added `breakpoint` prop to sub-components to use the configured mobile breakpoint setting.

5. Fixed mobile drawer transform direction to account for `drawerSide` setting (left vs right).

This ensures the navbar correctly shows desktop/mobile layouts based on the container width, not the browser viewport. When the page is viewed in the mobile breakpoint preview frame, the hamburger menu shows and desktop links hide - as expected.

---

## PreBuilt Navbar - Container Query Fix (NOT Viewport Media Queries)

There's a bug in the responsive behavior for the navbar. The navbar was using viewport-based approaches:

- Canvas editor (prebuilt-navbar.tsx): Tailwind's `md:hidden` and `hidden md:block` classes
- Preview mode (page-renderer.tsx): `@media (max-width: ...)` CSS media queries

This is wrong because the navbar might be placed inside any container - a frame, a different breakpoint preview, or any other parent. The viewport width is irrelevant. What matters is the WIDTH OF THE CONTAINER the navbar is inside.

**Solution**: Replace all viewport-based media queries with CSS Container Queries (`@container`):

1. Add `containerType: 'inline-size'` to the navbar wrapper so it becomes a container query context
2. Replace `@media (max-width: ${breakpoint}px)` with `@container (max-width: ${breakpoint}px)`
3. Replace `@media (min-width: ${breakpoint}px)` with `@container (min-width: ${breakpoint}px)`
4. Remove Tailwind's viewport-based classes (`md:hidden`, `hidden md:block`) and use container query CSS instead

The key insight: Container queries respond to the parent container's width, not the browser viewport. This is essential for a website builder where elements can be nested inside various containers at different widths.

---

## PreBuilt Navbar - Consistent Hardcoded Default Styles

The navbar sub-elements (logo, links, CTA button) were displaying different colors between the canvas editor and the preview renderer. This was confusing because:

- Canvas editor used Tailwind classes (`bg-primary`, `text-muted-foreground`)
- Preview renderer used hardcoded hex colors (`#3b82f6`, `white`)

This caused visual inconsistencies when switching between editor and preview modes.

**Solution**: Standardize on hardcoded inline styles for BOTH canvas editor AND preview renderer:

1. Canvas editor (prebuilt-navbar.tsx):

   - Converted all Tailwind classes to inline styles
   - Logo uses `color: 'inherit'` to pick up navbar container color
   - Links use `color: 'inherit'` with `opacity: 0.8`
   - CTA button uses variant-specific styles: `primary` (#3b82f6/white), `secondary` (transparent/inherit), `outline` (transparent/inherit/border)

2. Preview renderer (page-renderer.tsx):

   - Simplified sub-components to remove `styles` prop dependency
   - All components now use `color: 'inherit'` or the same hardcoded variant styles
   - Mobile drawer uses hardcoded dark background (#1a1a1a) with white text

3. Key pattern: Parent navbar sets `color` in its styles, children inherit via `color: 'inherit'`

This ensures visual parity between editor and preview. Users will later be able to customize these colors through the Settings panel in the properties sidebar.

NAVBAR COLOR CUSTOMIZATION AND DYNAMIC PAGE LINKS

Add color customization options to the navbar Settings panel and implement dynamic page-based navigation links.

Requirements:

1. COLOR CUSTOMIZATION IN SETTINGS TAB:

   - Add color options for individual navbar elements in the Settings panel
   - Logo: text color only (no background needed - it's part of the navbar)
   - Navigation Links: text color only (they're inline text, not buttons)
   - CTA Button: both background color AND text color

2. DYNAMIC PAGE-BASED NAVIGATION LINKS:
   - When a navbar is dragged and dropped onto the canvas, auto-populate with all existing website pages
   - Links should connect to internal pages using the page ID and slug
   - Users can switch between internal page links and custom URLs for each link
   - Users can add, remove, or reorder links as needed
   - This speeds up development significantly for users

1. Updated NavbarSettings types (types.ts):

   - Added `textColor?: string` to logo settings
   - Added `linksTextColor?: string` for all navigation links
   - Added `backgroundColor?: string` and `textColor?: string` to ctaButton
   - Added `pageId?: string` to NavbarLink for internal page linking

2. Updated navbar-settings.tsx:

   - LinkItem component now supports two modes: Page Link (dropdown) and Custom URL (text input)
   - Mode toggle button switches between page linking and custom URL modes
   - Added ColorPickerControl for logo text color, links text color, and CTA colors

3. Updated canvas.tsx:

   - On navbar drop, automatically generates links from existing scenes/pages
   - Each auto-generated link has pageId, label (page name), and href (page slug)
   - Falls back to default links if no pages exist

4. Updated prebuilt-navbar.tsx and page-renderer.tsx:
   - All color-related components now use settings values with fallback defaults
   - Logo uses `logo.textColor || 'inherit'`
   - Links use `linksTextColor || 'inherit'`
   - CTA button uses `ctaButton.backgroundColor` and `ctaButton.textColor` with variant-based fallbacks

Key Files Modified:

- `_components/properties-panel/navbar-settings.tsx` - Added color controls and page linking

SIDEBAR TABS ANIMATION AND MULTI-PANEL BUG FIX

There were two issues with the sidebar tabs:

1. UGLY ANIMATION:

   - The HoverCard component had multiple animations: fade-in, fade-out, zoom-in, zoom-out, and slide-in effects
   - These animations were distracting and felt clunky

2. MULTIPLE PANELS VISIBLE AT ONCE:
   - Each tab had its own independent HoverCard component
   - When hovering over different tabs, multiple panels could appear simultaneously
   - When a tab was pinned, hovering over other tabs still showed their panels

SOLUTION - Replace HoverCard with Single Controlled Panel:

Instead of using multiple HoverCards (one per tab), refactored to use a single panel:

1. Removed HoverCard dependency entirely
2. Added state to track panel visibility (`isPanelVisible`)
3. Tab hover shows the panel and switches content (if not pinned)
4. Tab click pins the panel open
5. Only ONE panel ever visible at a time - content switches instantly
6. When pinned, hovering other tabs does NOT change content (pinned tab stays active)
7. Mouse leave on entire sidebar area hides panel (with small delay to prevent flickering)
8. No animations - panel appears/disappears instantly

BEHAVIOR NOW:

- Hover any tab → panel appears with that tab's content
- Click any tab → panel is pinned with that tab's content
- When pinned, hovering other tabs highlights them but doesn't change panel content
- Click pinned tab again → unpins and hides panel
- Mouse leave sidebar area → panel hides (if not pinned)

Key Files Modified:

- `_components/sidebar/sidebar.tsx` - Replaced HoverCard with single controlled panel

---

PROPERTIES PANEL TAB EXPERIENCE - SAME AS SIDEBAR

Applied the same tab switching experience from the sidebar to the properties panel (Design/Settings tabs).

Previous Issues:

1. HoverCard had animations that looked clunky
2. Multiple panels could appear at the same time
3. When pinned, couldn't preview other tabs by hovering

Solution - Single Controlled Panel:

- Removed HoverCard dependency
- Single panel that shows content for the currently displayed tab
- Hover any tab to preview its content (even when another is pinned)
- Click any tab to pin it
- When mouse leaves, reverts to pinned tab content (or hides if nothing pinned)
- No animations for instant, clean feel

Behavior:

- Hover any tab → panel shows that tab's content
- Click any tab → pins that tab (blue dot indicator)
- When pinned + hover other tabs → shows hovered tab as preview
- Mouse leaves panel area → reverts to pinned tab content
- Click pinned tab again → unpins and hides panel
- No tab pinned + mouse leaves → panel hides

Key Files Modified:

- `_components/properties-panel/properties-panel.tsx` - Replaced HoverCard with single controlled panel

SIDEBAR AND PROPERTIES PANEL HOVER DETECTION FIX

Bug: When hovering over the icon bar tabs, the panel would open. But to close/revert the panel, the user had to move their cursor past the entire width of the opened panel. This was because the hover detection was on the outer container that wrapped both the icon bar AND the panel.

Fix: Separated the hover detection so that:

1. Icon bar has its own mouse enter/leave handlers
2. Panel content has its own mouse enter/leave handlers
3. Moving off the icon bar triggers the close/revert behavior
4. Moving into the panel keeps it open for interaction
5. Moving off the panel also triggers close/revert

Behavior now:

- Hover icon bar tab → panel opens with that tab's content
- Move cursor off icon bar (but not into panel) → panel closes/reverts after small delay
- Move cursor from icon bar into panel → panel stays open (timeout cancelled)
- Move cursor off panel → panel closes/reverts immediately
- When pinned, panel always stays visible, just reverts to pinned tab content

Key Files Modified:

- `_components/sidebar/sidebar.tsx` - Separated icon bar and panel hover handlers
- `_components/properties-panel/properties-panel.tsx` - Separated icon bar and panel hover handlers

LOCAL COMPONENTS SYSTEM - ARCHITECTURE PLANNING

Building a Local Components feature for the website builder. Components are local to the entire website (not domain, not page - the whole website). Users can create reusable components through any element on their pages.

Requirements Overview:

1. COMPONENT CREATION:
   - Any frame element can be converted into a component if it's not already a component
   - The "Convert to Component" option appears in the Settings tab for eligible elements
   - Components are saved at the website level and accessible across all pages

2. COMPONENT PURPOSE - CMS INTEGRATION:
   - The main purpose is to allow users to connect CMS data to component properties
   - In the future, users will connect CMS table fields to component settings/props
   - Example: A "Product Card" component with image, title, description, price, and button can be connected to a Products CMS table

3. COMPONENT SETTINGS/PROPS SYSTEM:
   - When inside a component, users can click on child elements to create custom properties
   - These properties map to any element property (from Design tab OR Settings tab)
   - Example: For an image element, properties like src, size, fit, corners, etc. can be exposed as component props
   - Example: For a button, link/action settings can be exposed as component props

4. COMPLEX PROPERTY HANDLING:
   - Some properties have multiple sub-values (like layout which has direction, alignment, wrap, etc.)
   - Some properties conditionally show/hide other properties (like responsive mode affects available options)
   - Need a source of truth architecture to handle these relationships cleanly

5. ARCHITECTURE REQUIREMENTS:
   - Need global, solid architecture to avoid complexity issues
   - Properties have different types (single values, multi-value groups, conditional visibility)
   - The system should be extensible for future CMS integration
   - Type safety throughout to prevent data mismatches

This is a complex feature requiring careful architectural planning before implementation to avoid messy code and edge cases.

____________________________________________________________________________

LOCAL COMPONENTS FEATURE - DETAILED ARCHITECTURE REQUEST

I want to build the Local Components feature inside this application. I've already set up the Components tab with a dropdown in the sidebar. Here's how this feature works:

SCOPE:
- Components are LOCAL to the entire WEBSITE only (not domain, not page - the whole website)
- For a website, there will be components - that's how this works

COMPONENT CREATION FLOW:
- Users can create components through any element on their pages
- Example: If they create a Card (a frame with elements inside it), they can click on it
- In the Settings tab, if the parent-most element is not already a component, they can convert that frame into a component

PURPOSE - CMS INTEGRATION:
- In the future, users will be able to add dynamic properties from CMS items into component fields
- Users can connect a specific CMS table to a component
- They can then connect each field from those CMS table elements into any property that accepts a value inside the component

EXAMPLE WORKFLOW:
1. User creates a Card with: image, title, description, price, and button
2. User converts this frame to a Component
3. User clicks on elements inside the Component to set up Settings (what React calls Props)
4. These settings are any property related to that element:
   - For an image: auto-width, source, size, fit, corners, options
   - Not just the Design tab - also the Settings tab properties can be created as custom properties
5. User can connect these custom property values to CMS table data
6. Example: Products CMS with titles, descriptions, prices, and buy links
   - Button will have link/action setting in the future
   - User connects button action to CMS product link
   - User connects price text to CMS price field

COMPLEX PROPERTY HANDLING:
- Some properties are made of multiple sub-properties
- Example: In the future, a button will have action type (link vs show popup, etc.)
- Example: Frame layout has multiple values (direction, wrap, alignment, etc.)
- Some properties conditionally show/hide other property values
- Example: When an element becomes responsive, certain properties are disclosed/hidden

I need guidance and help to create a global, solid architecture so we don't mess this up. It's a very complex feature and I want to get the foundation right before implementation.

____________________________________________________________________________

LOCAL COMPONENTS FEATURE - Continuation (Convert to Component Button & Component Instance Renderer)

Continuing the Local Components feature implementation. The core architecture was already established (property-registry.ts, component types, component-utils.ts, canvas-slice actions/selectors). This session focused on implementing the UI components:

1. Add "Convert to Component" button in the Settings panel for frame elements - Opens a dialog to name the component, then converts the frame and its children to a LocalComponent definition while replacing the original frame with a ComponentInstanceElement.

2. Update the Components sidebar tab to display local components - Shows a list of all LocalComponents for the website with drag-to-add functionality and delete option (only when no instances exist).

3. Create the ComponentInstanceElement renderer - This is crucial because component instances have NON-EDITABLE children. The instance itself can be selected, moved, and resized, but children are rendered from the component's sourceTree and cannot be individually edited. This enforces consistency across all component instances.

Key architectural decisions:
- Component instances store only: position, size, componentId, and propValues
- Children are rendered dynamically from the component definition's sourceTree
- propValues are applied using applyPropValuesToElements() at render time
- Component children have pointerEvents: none to prevent interaction
- Visual feedback shows a purple tint for selected component instances

____________________________________________________________________________

LOCAL COMPONENTS - CRITICAL BUGS FIX (Database Persistence & Deep Cloning)

Multiple critical bugs were identified with the Local Components feature that needed to be fixed:

BUG #1 - Components Not Persisted to Database:
- Components were only stored in Redux state (page-level memory)
- They would disappear on page refresh or navigation
- Components need to be saved at the WEBSITE level in the database
- Components need to be available across all pages within the same website

BUG #2 - Component Creation Added to Undo/Redo History:
- Creating a component added it to the canvas undo/redo stack
- This was wrong because components live at website level, not canvas level
- Undoing on canvas should NOT undo component creation
- Component operations should be immediate and permanent (bypassing undo/redo)

BUG #3 - Child Element Styles Lost on Conversion:
- When converting a frame to a component, only the root frame's styles were preserved
- Text, button, image element styles were all wiped
- All nested children lost their styles
- Root cause: Shallow cloning with spread operator did not preserve nested style objects

FIXES IMPLEMENTED:

1. Deep Cloning (Bug #3):
   - Added `deepCloneElement()` function using JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) for complete deep copy
   - Updated `convertFrameToComponent()` to use deep cloning for rootElement and all childElements
   - This preserves all nested style objects during component conversion

2. Database Schema (Bug #1):
   - Added LocalComponent model to Prisma schema with fields:
     - id, websiteId, name, description, sourceTree (JSON), exposedProps (JSON), tags, createdAt, updatedAt
   - Added relation to Website model with cascade delete
   - Created local-component.service.ts as the Data Access Layer for database operations

3. tRPC Router (Bug #1):
   - Created localComponentsRouter with CRUD procedures:
     - create: Creates component in database (returns real database ID)
     - getByWebsite: Fetches all components for a website
     - getById: Gets single component by ID
     - update: Updates component fields
     - delete: Removes component from database

4. useLocalComponents Hook (Bugs #1 & #2):
   - Created custom hook that bridges Redux state with database
   - Loads components from database on mount using tRPC query
   - Provides createComponent(), updateComponent(), deleteComponent() functions
   - These functions save to database first, then update Redux
   - Bypasses undo/redo system since component operations are website-level
   - Handles optimistic updates and error recovery

5. UI Integration:
   - Updated FrameSettingsSection to use useLocalComponents hook instead of direct Redux dispatch
   - Component creation now persists to database before updating Redux
   - Shows loading state during async component creation

Key files created/modified:
- schema.prisma - Added LocalComponent model
- local-component.service.ts - Database operations
- local-components.ts (tRPC router) - API endpoints
- use-local-components.ts - React hook for database sync
- component-utils.ts - Added deepCloneElement function
- properties-panel.tsx - Updated to use the hook

This ensures components are properly persisted at the website level, available across all pages, and have their styles fully preserved during conversion.

____________________________________________________________________________

BUG #5 - Properties Panel Shows Wrong Controls for Instances:
- Component instances were showing the full Design tab with all design controls
- This was incorrect because instance styles are controlled by the master component
- Users should only be able to modify exposed prop values, not the instance's visual design

FIX IMPLEMENTED:

  "Component Instance - Styles are controlled by the master component. Use the Settings tab to customize exposed properties."

  - Each property shows its current value and can be toggled to expose/unexpose
  - Exposed props can be renamed with inline editing
  - The UI uses Link2/Unlink2 icons for clear visual feedback

Key principle: Component instances have NON-EDITABLE children and fixed styling. The only customization allowed is through exposed props, which can be set per instance. This maintains consistency across all instances while allowing controlled customization.

LOCAL COMPONENTS - Canvas Rendering & Database-Redux Sync

Fixed critical issues where components were not rendering and not showing in the sidebar:

PROBLEM 1 - Component Instances Not Rendering:
- The canvas was correctly configured to render `component` type elements
- However, the `selectLocalComponentById` selector was returning null
- This happened because components were never being loaded from the database into Redux

PROBLEM 2 - Components Not Showing in Sidebar:
- The LocalComponentsPanel was reading from Redux state
- But the useLocalComponents hook (which fetches from database) was not being called

SOLUTION - Proper Database-Redux Sync:

1. Added useLocalComponents hook to Canvas component:
   - The hook is now called when the Canvas first renders
   - This fetches components from the database via tRPC
   - Automatically populates Redux state with the fetched components
   - Component instances on the canvas can now find their definitions

2. Updated LocalComponentsPanel to use the hook:
   - Added useLocalComponents hook call to trigger data fetch
   - Added loading state while components are being fetched
   - Updated delete handler to use the database-synced delete function
   - Components now properly display in the sidebar

3. Updated page-renderer.tsx for published pages:
   - Added ComponentInstanceRenderer for rendering component instances
   - Added ComponentChildRenderer for recursive child rendering
   - Added optional `components` prop to PageRendererProps
   - This allows published pages to pass in components for rendering
   - Fixed style computation function signatures (isPage, options with breakpoint)

TWO-WAY SYNC FLOW:
1. On builder load: useLocalComponents fetches from DB -> loads into Redux
2. On component creation: Creates in DB first -> adds to Redux
3. On component update: Updates Redux immediately -> syncs to DB
4. On component delete: Deletes from DB -> removes from Redux

The useLocalComponents hook is the single source of truth for database operations.
Redux is the source of truth for rendering (fast access).
Database is the source of truth for persistence (durable storage).

Files modified:
- canvas.tsx - Added useLocalComponents hook call for component loading
- local-components-panel.tsx - Added useLocalComponents for loading and delete sync
- page-renderer.tsx - Added ComponentInstanceRenderer and ComponentChildRenderer

____________________________________________________________________________

---

LOCAL COMPONENTS - Drag and Drop from Sidebar

Fixed the ability to drag local components from the sidebar onto the canvas.

PROBLEM:
- The canvas handleDrop function only checked for `application/json` drag data
- Local components use `application/x-local-component` MIME type
- Components could not be dropped onto the canvas

SOLUTION:
Updated canvas.tsx handleDrop function to handle component drops:

1. Check for `application/x-local-component` data FIRST (before JSON check)
2. If component ID is present:
   - Look up the component from Redux using selectLocalComponentById
   - Create a new ComponentInstanceElement using createComponentInstance
   - Add the instance to the canvas at the drop location
   - Select the new instance

The drop creates a ComponentInstanceElement with:
- Unique ID
- Position at drop coordinates (converted from screen to canvas coords)
- Reference to the component via componentId
- Empty propValues (uses component defaults)
- parentId: null (root element)

Files modified:
- canvas.tsx - Added component drop handling in handleDrop function

____________________________________________________________________________

LOCAL COMPONENTS - Child Element Properties Not Preserved

Fixed an issue where child element properties were not being preserved when converting frames to components.

PROBLEM:
- When converting a frame to a component, only CSS styles were being rendered for child elements
- Element properties like autoWidth, autoHeight, container, responsive, objectFit, etc. were ignored
- This caused child frames, texts, images, and buttons to render incorrectly
- For example, a frame with autoWidth=true would render with a fixed pixel width instead

ROOT CAUSE:
The ChildRenderer (in component-instance-element.tsx) and ComponentChildRenderer (in page-renderer.tsx) components were:
1. Hardcoding wrapper styles with fixed element.width and element.height
2. Not reading element properties like autoWidth, autoHeight, container, responsive
3. Not using the proper compute functions with all their logic

SOLUTION:
Updated both child renderers to properly respect ALL element properties:

1. TEXT ELEMENTS: Now respect autoWidth (100% vs fixed) and autoHeight (auto vs fixed)
2. IMAGE ELEMENTS: Now respect autoWidth and objectFit property
3. BUTTON ELEMENTS: Now respect autoWidth and autoHeight properties
4. FRAME ELEMENTS: Now respect autoWidth, container, responsive, and flexWrap properties

The fix ensures component instances render identically to the original elements by:
- Using the same compute style functions (computeTextContentStyles, computeFrameContentStyles, etc.)
- Reading all element properties (not just styles)
- Applying container mode, responsive mode, and wrap behavior correctly

Files modified:
- component-instance-element.tsx - Updated ChildRenderer to respect all element properties
- page-renderer.tsx - Updated ComponentChildRenderer to respect all element properties

____________________________________________________________________________

LOCAL COMPONENTS - Master vs Instance Distinction

Implemented visual and functional distinction between master components and their instances.

ARCHITECTURE UNDERSTANDING:
- Master Component: The LocalComponent definition stored in the database with sourceTree
- Instance: A ComponentInstanceElement on canvas that REFERENCES the master via componentId
- Instances are NOT copies - they point to the master definition
- When the master updates, all instances should update (future feature)

PRIMARY INSTANCE ID:
Added primaryInstanceId to LocalComponent to track the "master" instance on canvas:
- The first instance created during conversion is the primary/master instance
- It has special status - it represents the source of the component
- Cannot be detached without special handling (would orphan other instances)

UI CHANGES:
1. Master Instance shows:
   - Purple gradient background with "MASTER" badge
   - Violet icon styling
   - Info message explaining master component behavior
   - NO detach button (master cannot be detached)

2. Regular Instance shows:
   - Standard styling with "Instance" badge
   - "References master component" subtitle
   - Detach button available to convert back to frame

DATABASE CHANGES:
- Added primaryInstanceId column to LocalComponent model in Prisma schema
- Stored and retrieved through tRPC router and service layer

Files modified:
- types.ts - Added primaryInstanceId to LocalComponent interface
- prisma/schema.prisma - Added primaryInstanceId column
- local-component.service.ts - Updated create function and select fields
- local-components.ts (tRPC) - Added primaryInstanceId to create schema
- use-local-components.ts - Added primaryInstanceId to CreateComponentInput and transformations
- component-utils.ts - Set primaryInstanceId when converting frame to component
- properties-panel.tsx - Added Master vs Instance UI with conditional rendering

____________________________________________________________________________

## Auto-Save Master Component Changes to Database

PURPOSE: When master components are edited on the canvas, automatically sync changes to the LocalComponent in the database.

PROMPT:
When I update one of the master components, it doesn't update inside the database for the local component. When I update an element, you need to smartly sense it during the save process. If the master component was updated, you need to not only let the canvas get updated, but also update that master component's new properties in the database. That's the whole reason we created this master component system.

We don't need an "Edit Component" mode at all in this case because the canvas will sense the local master component changed, and then you can do the saving logic.

Don't individually check each style property. Just replace the entire sourceTree.rootElement with the updated element. Keep it simple.

IMPLEMENTATION:
Two-part solution:

1. REDUX SYNC (canvas-slice.ts - updateElement reducer):
   When a primary instance (master component) is updated on the canvas, the updateElement
   reducer now also updates LocalComponent.sourceTree.rootElement with the new element data.
   This keeps Redux in sync immediately.

2. DATABASE SYNC (use-component-auto-save.ts hook):
   Watches localComponents in Redux for changes and syncs them to the database via tRPC.
   Uses debounced saves to prevent rapid API calls.

KEY CODE in updateElement reducer:
```typescript
if (existing.type === 'component') {
  const instance = page.canvas.elements[id]
  const componentId = (existing as any).componentId
  const component = state.localComponents[componentId]

  if (component && component.primaryInstanceId === id) {
    // Replace the rootElement with the updated instance
    state.localComponents[componentId] = {
      ...component,
      sourceTree: {
        ...component.sourceTree,
        rootElement: {
          ...component.sourceTree.rootElement,
          ...instance,
          id: component.sourceTree.rootElement.id,
          type: component.sourceTree.rootElement.type,
        },
      },
      updatedAt: Date.now(),
    }
  }
}
```

Files created:
- _hooks/use-component-auto-save.ts - Database sync hook

Files modified:
- _lib/canvas-slice.ts - updateElement reducer syncs master to sourceTree
- _hooks/index.ts - Exported useComponentAutoSave
- _components/canvas.tsx - Integrated useComponentAutoSave hook

____________________________________________________________________________

---

## Master Component Editability - Keep as Regular Frame

**WHAT:** Fix master component to be fully editable like a regular frame

**MY PROMPT:** 
I have a better, much better solution here. Just remove the prevention of editing on the master component. Look at how a regular element would be - if a regular element would have all of the properties, all of the designs and all the child elements can be selected, drag, drop, all that kind of stuff. So just let the master component be as such. That means I can change all of that. We don't need to have some sort of edit mode or anything. It's just like a regular element, but that component itself will be stored inside our local components database.

Creating a component means the whole component has to become a component, that's it, and it becomes a source tree like a reusable component. It's not a component instance, obviously. Component instance is when I drag it from the sidebar.

When I drag an instance from a sidebar that becomes a component instance. That renders its children from the source tree, not editable. Not just children - the entire element, everything about that element just points directly to the master component. It's a true instance and it reads from the source only.

Keep in mind we have property overrides for the children - that means you have to also use property overrides where we exposed some properties for the instance elements so that way it can still use its own values but it still refers to the original. If I remove a child called "text" from the master component, that will immediately delete the property override and the text element on the instance. But if I have a text element on the master and on the instance, I change the value through the expose property in the settings tab to a random value, that will obviously only affect that instance.

## Master Component Sync Bug Debug Session

**What this was about:** Debugging a bug where master component frames were not saving correctly - the master frame would revert to old state while instances and children would update correctly.

**User prompt (cleaned up):** The master component does not update, but instance components update perfectly. This means there's some sort of caching issue or ignore issue with the master component preventing it from being saved. On page refresh, grabbing the edge of a master component causes it to immediately flicker to the size of its instances - indicating there's some connection remembered but no sync happening.

**Actions taken:**
1. Added comprehensive debug logging to trace data flow between canvas elements, sourceTree, and database saves
2. Logging added to: BuilderClient (page load), useLocalComponents (component load), useMasterComponentSync (canvas → sourceTree sync), useComponentAutoSave (component save), useAutoSave (page save)
3. The logs will help identify where the data diverges and which save operation is using stale data



---

## Master Component Save Race Condition Bug Fix

**User Prompt:**
The master component does not update, the instance components perfectly update. So there is some sort of caching or ignore issue with the master component preventing it from being saved. When refreshing the page and grabbing the edge of a master component, it immediately flickers to the size of its instances. Somewhere it remembers some connection but there is no sync.

**Root Cause:**
useComponentAutoSave was initializing with 0 components, then when useLocalComponents loaded from DB, the auto-save hook saw this as a "change" (no lastSyncedKey existed) and immediately saved the OLD sourceTree back to DB - BEFORE useMasterComponentSync could sync the canvas state.

**Solution:**
Added logic to useComponentAutoSave to NOT save components that are just loaded from DB. Instead, store their key as baseline and wait for actual user edits. The fix detects first-seen components (lastSyncedKey === undefined) and stores baseline instead of triggering immediate save.




---

## Master Component NEVER Updates - Race Condition Fix

**User Prompt:**
The bug still exists. The master component NEVER updates itself quite literally.

**Root Cause:**
useMasterComponentSync had a race condition:
1. Canvas data loads synchronously (has master frames with masterOfComponentId)
2. useMasterComponentSync runs immediately on mount
3. BUT LocalComponents have not loaded yet (async tRPC query)
4. The sync is SKIPPED because localComponents[componentId] is undefined
5. Later, useLocalComponents loads components from DB
6. BUT useMasterComponentSync does NOT re-run because elements reference did not change - it exits at the fast path check

**Solution:**
Added localComponents to the fast-path reference check. Now when LocalComponents load from DB, the hook detects the change and re-evaluates all master frames, triggering the canvas-to-sourceTree sync that was previously skipped.




---

## Master Component Canvas Data Not Reloading After Refresh

**User Prompt:**
The master component is STILL NOT SAVING! It never updates.

**Root Cause (from server logs):**
The component sourceTree WAS being saved correctly to the database (logs showed correct width being saved). BUT the PAGE canvas data was NOT being reloaded on refresh.

The module-level `loadedPageId` variable in builder-client.tsx persisted across page refreshes in Next.js dev mode (HMR). On refresh:
1. loadedPageId was still set to the same pageId from before
2. The condition `loadedPageId !== pageId` was FALSE
3. Redux was NOT repopulated with fresh data from DB
4. The OLD canvas data stayed in Redux showing incorrect master frame dimensions

**Solution:**
Changed from tracking just pageId to tracking a dataKey that includes both pageId AND the page updatedAt timestamp. Added updatedAt field to builder.getDataById query return. Now when fresh data arrives from DB with a new timestamp, the data is reloaded into Redux.



---

## Fix React setState-during-render Bug in Website Builder

**Prompt:**
Fix the bug where going out of focus and coming back after a minute causes "Cannot update a component (PreviewOverlay) while rendering a different component (BuilderContent)" error. This happens in the website builder canvas. Do not break existing functionality.

**Context:** The root cause was dispatching Redux actions synchronously during the render phase in BuilderContent. When the browser tab loses focus and regains it, tRPC refetches data, triggering a re-render that dispatched during render - causing PreviewOverlay (which subscribes to Redux) to try to update while BuilderContent was still rendering.

**Solution:** Moved Redux dispatch from render phase to useEffect, and used useSyncExternalStore to subscribe to Redux hydration state for loading UI - eliminating the need for local useState that could cause cascading renders.


---

## Fix Detach Instance Feature for Component Instances

**Prompt:**
Fix up the Detach Instance feature for component instances. When the user clicks this, the element should detach as a "component" and become just a regular frame with editable children. Make sure that undoing also works.

**Context:** The previous implementation only converted the component instance to a frame type but didn't add any of the child elements. There was even a TODO comment: "This needs to use addElement for each child - For now, we'll handle this in a future iteration."

**Solution:** Created a new `detachComponentInstance` Redux action in canvas-slice.ts that atomically:
1. Replaces the component instance with a frame element (same ID preserves tree position)
2. Adds all child elements from the component's sourceTree with new IDs
3. Rebuilds the childrenMap for proper parent-child relationships
4. Unregisters the instance from the component's instanceIds

All operations happen in a SINGLE history entry, so Cmd+Z (undo) restores the component instance with all its original state perfectly.

## Fix Detached Instance Still Showing Purple Master Component State

**Prompt:**
Detach works but the purple states still exist, and for some reason it's considered as a master component after detaching.

**Context:** When detaching a component instance, the `detachComponentInstance` utility was spreading the root element from the component's sourceTree. That root element had `masterOfComponentId` property (since it's the master), which carried over to the detached frame, making it still appear as a master component with purple styling.

**Solution:** Updated the `detachComponentInstance` function in `component-utils.ts` to explicitly destructure and remove all component-related properties before creating the frame element:
- `masterOfComponentId` - Makes element appear as "master component" (purple state)
- `componentId` - Links to a component definition  
- `propValues` - Component instance prop values

Applied the same cleanup to all child elements as well.


---

## Fix Canvas Panning to Random Location on Window Focus

**Prompt:**
When I go off focus of the browser and come back into focus, for some reason the page pans to a random location. The refetching happens but why is the pan getting affected?

**Context:** When tRPC refetches data on window focus regain (`refetchOnWindowFocus`), the database returns viewport with default values (`panX: 0, panY: 0, zoom: 1`). The `loadPages` action was replacing the entire state including the viewport, which reset the user's current view position.

**Solution:** Updated the `loadPages` action in `canvas-slice.ts` to detect if this is a refetch (existing pages already loaded) vs first load. On refetch, it preserves the current viewport, selection, and history for each page instead of overwriting them with database values. This ensures:
- Viewport (pan/zoom) stays where the user left it
- Selection is not lost on refetch  
- Undo/redo history is preserved

## Fix Frame Wrap Property Not Transferring to Component Instances

**User Prompt:**
Everything works perfectly well, but there is one bug. The bug is that the properties of the frame, such as wrap, do not get converted with the component. Only the wrap is breaking. Most of the other things are being converted, but wrap on the frame for some reason on the master component does not go to its instances. Please fix this bug to ensure that all properties are 100% being passed in at all times. This is not related to the exposed property. I'm just talking about the styling for the master component itself. When a master component has some styles, all of its frame styles, as well as all of its children's, nested children's, all those styles should also be put in. So it looks like something might be wrong here. Please fix this.

**Solution:**
The bug was NOT in the sourceTree creation (which correctly uses `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify())` to deep clone all properties). The bug was in how component instances RENDER the root element. The `component-instance-element.tsx` and `page-renderer.tsx` were hardcoding `height: '100%'` and `height: rootFrame.height` without checking if `flexWrap: 'wrap'` was enabled. When wrap is enabled, height must be `'auto'` so the frame can grow with wrapped content.

**Files Modified:**
- `component-instance-element.tsx`: Added logic to detect `flexWrap: 'wrap'` and `responsive` on root frame, then use `height: 'auto'` for wrapper and content styles
- `page-renderer.tsx`: Same fix applied for preview/published rendering


---

## Fix Renderers Not Rendering Component Instances

**User Prompt:**
Now let's fix our renderers because all renderers do not render out custom components. They only render out regular elements. So if I put an instance of a component or master component, it doesn't matter if I put that on the root page element or on the renderer view, it shows as "component not found".

**Solution:**
The `PageRenderer` component has a `components` prop for passing LocalComponent definitions, but none of the call sites were providing it. Component instances need access to the LocalComponent definitions to render their sourceTree.

**Files Modified:**

1. **`preview-overlay.tsx`**:
   - Added `selectLocalComponents` import
   - Added `useAppSelector(selectLocalComponents)` to get components from Redux
   - Passed `components={localComponents}` to `PageRenderer`

2. **`breakpoint-mobile-frame.tsx`**:
   - Added `LocalComponent` type import
   - Added `components?: Record<string, LocalComponent>` to props interface
   - Passed `components={components}` to `PageRenderer`

3. **`canvas.tsx`**:
   - Added `selectLocalComponents` import
   - Added `useAppSelector(selectLocalComponents)` to get components from Redux
   - Passed `components={localComponents}` to `BreakpointMobileFrame`

4. **`page.service.ts`**:
   - Added `websiteId: true` to select in `getPublishedPage`

5. **`websites.ts` (tRPC router)**:
   - Added `localComponentService` import
   - Modified `getPublishedPage` to fetch components from database using `websiteId`
   - Returns `components` map in response

6. **`[domain]/[pathname]/page.tsx`**:
   - Added `LocalComponent` type import
   - Cast `pageData.components` to proper type
   - Passed `components={components}` to `PageRenderer`

**Architecture Notes:**
- For **builder canvas**: Components available via Redux (`selectLocalComponents`)
- For **preview/published pages**: Components passed as prop to `PageRenderer`
- `ComponentInstanceRenderer` uses `components[element.componentId]` to find the definition
- Without components, instances show "Component not found" error state

## Fix Mobile Justify Center Not Showing in Preview Renderer

**User Prompt:**
I found another bug when I set a frame on mobile properties to have justify center for some reason. It does not show inside the preview renderer. But it shows inside the mobile breakpoint renderer. Whatever is being rendered in there, it perfectly shows the mobile style being applied. But if I look inside the preview renderer, it doesn't show. What's going on here? Can you please fix this bug as well?

**Root Cause:**
The mobile breakpoint frame passes `breakpoint="mobile"` to PageRenderer, which directly merges responsive styles via `mergeResponsiveStyles()`. The preview overlay uses `breakpoint="desktop"` (default), relying on CSS container queries for mobile styles.

The CSS container queries were targeting `[data-element-id]` which is on the **outer wrapper div**, but flex properties like `justifyContent`, `alignItems`, `flexDirection` are applied to the **inner content div** in the FrameElementRenderer.

**Solution:**
1. Added `data-element-content={element.id}` attribute to the inner content div in both `PageElementRenderer` and `FrameElementRenderer`
2. Updated `generateElementResponsiveCSS()` in `responsive-css-generator.ts` to:
   - Target `[data-element-id]` for setting-based properties (width, height, visibility)
   - Target `[data-element-content]` for style-based properties (flex, padding, colors) on frame/page elements
   - Non-frame elements still use `[data-element-id]` since they don't have the two-div structure

**Files Modified:**

1. **`page-renderer.tsx`**:
   - Added `data-element-content={element.id}` to PageElementRenderer inner div (line 365)
   - Added `data-element-content={element.id}` to FrameElementRenderer inner div (line 576)

2. **`responsive-css-generator.ts`**:
   - Updated `generateElementResponsiveCSS()` to generate separate CSS rules:
     - Setting declarations → `[data-element-id]` (wrapper)
     - Style declarations → `[data-element-content]` for frames/pages, `[data-element-id]` for others (content)

**Architecture Notes:**
- Frame/Page elements have a two-div structure: wrapper (position/size) + content (flex styles)
- CSS container queries must target the correct div for each property type
- Mobile breakpoint frame works because it passes `breakpoint="mobile"` directly to style computation
- Preview overlay relies on CSS container queries which need the right selectors

## Nested Component Instance Selection in Properties Panel

**Prompt:**
The user reported that nested component instances within composed components can be hovered (showing hover state) but cannot be selected. When clicking on nested instance children, they do not get selected and their exposed props cannot be edited.

**Solution:**
The issue was in the PropertiesPanel component. When a scoped ID (format: `parentInstanceId::nestedElementId`) was selected, the lookup in `activePage.canvas.elements[lastId]` returned null because scoped IDs are virtual elements that do not exist in the canvas elements dictionary.

The fix involved:
1. Detecting if the selected ID contains the scope delimiter `::`
2. Parsing the scoped ID to extract parent instance ID and nested element ID
3. Getting the parent component instance from canvas.elements
4. Getting the component definition to find the nested element in sourceTree
5. Creating a virtual ComponentInstanceElement with the scoped ID and merged propValues
6. The virtual element has propValues that combine sourceTree defaults with parent nestedPropValues overrides

This enables nested component instances to be selected and their exposed properties edited independently per parent instance.




---

## Fix: getElementById Not Resolving Scoped IDs for Nested Instance Selection

**Prompt:**
User reported that clicking on nested instance children inside a composed component instance does not select them. The hover state shows correctly but clicking does not work.

**Root Cause:**
The `getElementById` function in `canvas.tsx` only looked up elements from `canvas.elements` dictionary. Scoped IDs (format: `parentInstanceId::nestedElementId`) do not exist in this dictionary because they are virtual elements rendered from the component sourceTree. When `useDrag` hook called `getElementById` with a scoped ID, it returned `undefined`, causing the selection logic to abort early.

**Solution:**
Updated `getElementById` in `canvas.tsx` to handle scoped IDs:
1. Detect if the ID contains the scope delimiter `::`
2. Parse to extract parent instance ID and nested element ID
3. Get parent component instance from canvas.elements
4. Get component definition from localComponents in Redux state
5. Find nested element in the component sourceTree
6. Return a virtual ComponentInstanceElement with:
   - The scoped ID for proper selection tracking
   - parentId set to the parent instance ID (critical for ancestor chain/selection cycling)
   - Merged propValues from sourceTree defaults and parent nestedPropValues

The key insight was that the virtual element parentId must be set to the parent component instance ID, not the sourceTree parentId. This ensures the useDrag selection cycling logic works correctly by building the proper ancestor chain.

## Fix 3 Bugs in Mochi AI Chat Route and Wallet Service

Fix 3 bugs in the Mochi AI chat route and wallet service. Write good comments above every block/function. Never use any or unknown types. Never touch git, migrations, prisma generate. Add SOURCE OF TRUTH keywords where applicable.

Bug 1 - Wallet Charging Silent Failure (HIGH): The fire-and-forget charging in route.ts silently swallows failures. Add a recordFailedAICharge() function to wallet.service.ts that creates a FAILED WalletTransaction for audit/recovery. In route.ts, replace the fire-and-forget block with retry logic (3 attempts with exponential backoff) and call recordFailedAICharge if all retries fail to create an audit trail.

Bug 6 - Background Mode Prompt Mismatch (MEDIUM): The server injects an impossible instruction telling the AI to modify tool results (which it can't do). Replace that instruction with something the AI CAN follow - telling it not to mention navigation or opening builders since content is being created in the background.

Bug 10 - No Timeout Error Differentiation (LOW): AbortSignal.timeout throws a generic AbortError. Create a custom AbortController with a setTimeout that aborts with a custom reason (GENERATION_TIMEOUT). In the catch block, check for this and return a specific error response with the MOCHI_ERROR_CODES.TIMEOUT code. Clean up the timeout when the stream completes to prevent memory leaks.
